ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai boat noodle with grilled chicken. FOODON_00002572 (noodle soup food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for Thai boat noodle soup as a composite dish. A0FHR (cooked chicken meat) is used as the closest parent anchor for the dominant protein component. The dish also contains noodles and broth.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Thai grilled chicken boat noodle as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Thai grilled chicken boat noodle, approximately 450 g, comprising broth, noodles, grilled chicken pieces, and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Boat noodle soup with chicken (ก๋วยเตี๋ยวเรือไก่) — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore nutrient data for noodle soups
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO-INFOODS / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source contains a direct entry for Thai grilled chicken boat noodle as a composite dish. Values are estimated by component analysis: rice noodles (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #20050), grilled chicken breast (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #05064), pork-blood-based broth (FAO/INFOODS SEA analogous broth entries), and vegetables. Weighted average per 100 g of assembled dish at approximately 450 g serving. HPB Singapore noodle soup entries used as a plausibility cross-check. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice noodles and mixed noodle soup analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai grilled chicken boat noodle. GI of 53 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles in soup context (GI ~53, low category), which is the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source in this dish. The grilled chicken and broth components contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 50.4) / 100 ≈ 26.7, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 11.2 × 4.5 = 50.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from broth and fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium broth, as a single 450 g bowl provides approximately 1890 mg sodium, which approaches or exceeds the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: The rice noodle base contributes a glycemic load of approximately 26.7 per bowl, which is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables or reduce noodle quantity to lower postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from broth and chicken skin
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content and moderate saturated fat (approximately 3.2 g per bowl) are relevant for cardiovascular risk management; choosing skinless grilled chicken and requesting low-sodium broth can meaningfully reduce cardiovascular burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Grilled Chicken Boat Noodle (ก๋วยเตี๋ยวเรือไก่ย่าง, romanized: Kuai Tiao Ruea Kai Yang) is a Thai noodle soup dish that combines the traditional boat noodle (kuai tiao ruea) format with grilled chicken as the primary protein. Traditional Thai boat noodles originated from vendors selling noodles from small boats along the canals of central Thailand, particularly in Ayutthaya and Bangkok. The broth is characteristically dark, rich, and intensely flavoured, typically made from pork or chicken stock seasoned with fish sauce, dark soy sauce, dried spices, and sometimes pork blood, which gives it a deep umami character. In this grilled chicken variant, the chicken is marinated with Thai herbs and spices — commonly lemongrass, garlic, coriander root, and turmeric — then grilled to produce a smoky, charred exterior with juicy interior meat. The dish is served over springy rice noodles (sen lek or sen yai) with accompaniments such as bean sprouts, morning glory (water spinach), fried garlic, and dried chilli flakes. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 98 kcal, 8.5 g protein, 2.8 g fat, and 11.2 g carbohydrate, making it a moderate-energy, protein-rich noodle soup. The sodium content is high at approximately 420 mg per 100 g (around 1890 mg per bowl), which is a primary clinical concern for hypertensive individuals. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low category) based on the rice noodle component, though the glycemic load per bowl is moderate at approximately 26.7. This dish is suitable for individuals seeking a high-protein, lower-fat Thai meal but requires sodium awareness for those managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with fish or soy allergies.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source contains a direct nutrient entry for Thai grilled chicken boat noodle as a composite dish. Values are estimated by component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for rice noodles and grilled chicken, FAO/INFOODS SEA analogous broth data, and HPB Singapore noodle soup entries as a plausibility cross-check. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database rice noodle analogue. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from component analysis; no direct approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. 2. The following micronutrients are reported as -1 (sentinel value for not reported) because they are not reliably reported across the analogous source entries used for estimation (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FAO/INFOODS SEA, HPB Singapore) and cannot be defensibly assigned without a direct measurement: vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries; vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries; vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries; folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries; vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries; vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries; phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries; magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries; zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries; selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0; this is confirmed as negligible/trace for grilled chicken and rice noodle preparations per USDA SR Legacy data. 4. The dish is not certified halal at the restaurant level (Saap Saap Thai - Funan); halal status is marked false pending verification. 5. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodles in soup context from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists. 6. Sodium content is high and represents the primary clinical concern for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. 7. The food_name has been normalised from the menu-style 'N8 Grilled Chicken Boat Noodle' to the canonical 'Thai Grilled Chicken Boat Noodle'; the original menu name is preserved in aliases.
